quiz.title: Unit 10 Quiz 11, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Net Ionic Equations and Solution Classification
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Net Ionic Equations, Spectator Ions, Saturated/Unsaturated/Supersaturated Solutions, Solubility Rules, Conductivity, Intermolecular Forces

questions.question: Which of the following is not a net ionic equation?
questions.explanation: Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) → NaCl(aq) is not a net ionic equation because NaCl is soluble and remains as ions in solution. This equation doesn't represent a chemical reaction at all. The other options show actual precipitation or neutralization reactions with spectator ions removed.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a net ionic equation?
questions.explanation: Zn(s) + CuCl₂(aq) → ZnCl₂(aq) + Cu(s) is a full molecular equation, not a net ionic equation. It includes spectator ions (Cl⁻) that should be canceled out in a net ionic equation.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a net ionic equation?
questions.explanation: AgNO₃(aq) + NaCl(aq) → AgCl(s) + NaNO₃(aq) is a full molecular equation. The net ionic equation would be Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) → AgCl(s), with NO₃⁻ and Na⁺ as spectator ions.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a net ionic equation?
questions.explanation: 2K⁺(aq) + 2Cl⁻(aq) + Pb²⁺(aq) + 2NO₃⁻(aq) → PbCl₂(s) + 2K⁺(aq) + 2NO₃⁻(aq) includes spectator ions (K⁺ and NO₃⁻) on both sides. The net ionic equation should be Pb²⁺(aq) + 2Cl⁻(aq) → PbCl₂(s).

questions.question: Which of the following is not a net ionic equation?
questions.explanation: HCl(aq) + NaOH(aq) → NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l) is a full molecular equation. The net ionic equation would be H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l), with Na⁺ and Cl⁻ as spectator ions.

questions.question: A solution is saturated at room temperature, 25°C. Afterwards, it is cooled to 20°C with no change to the appearance of the liquid. How would we describe this solution?
questions.explanation: When a saturated solution is cooled and no precipitate forms, it contains more solute than normally possible at the lower temperature. This is called a supersaturated solution.

questions.question: A saturated solution of KNO₃ at 20°C contains 32 g of solute in 100 g of water. If a student dissolves 15 g of KNO₃ in 50 g of water at the same temperature, how is the solution classified?
questions.explanation: At saturation, 50 g of water can dissolve 16 g of KNO₃ (half of 32 g). Since only 15 g is dissolved, the solution is unsaturated.

questions.question: The molar solubility of Silver Chloride (AgCl) is $1.33 \times 10^{-5}\text{ M}$ at $25\text{°C}$. Calculate the $K_{sp}$ for $AgCl$.
questions.explanation:
AgCl dissociates as: AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq)
Ksp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻] = s × s = s²
Ksp = (1.33 × 10⁻⁵)² = 1.77 × 10⁻¹⁰

questions.question: The molar solubility of Calcium Fluoride (CaF₂) is $2.1 \times 10^{-4}\text{ M}$ at a specific temperature. Calculate the $K_{sp}$.
The solubility of NaCl is 36.0 g per 100 mL of water. A solution is prepared by adding 9.0 g of NaCl to 25 mL of water. Classify the solution.
questions.explanation: For 25 mL of water, the maximum amount of NaCl that can dissolve is (36.0 g/100 mL) × 25 mL = 9.0 g. Exactly 9.0 g is added, so the solution is saturated.

questions.question: Which of the following tests can be used to distinguish between an ionic solution and most molecular solutions?
questions.explanation: Ionic solutions conduct electricity due to the presence of mobile ions, while most molecular solutions do not conduct electricity well. Conductivity tests can distinguish between them.

questions.question: Which type(s) of molecule(s) are polar solvents more likely to be able to dissolve?
questions.explanation: According to the principle 'like dissolves like', polar solvents are good at dissolving polar and ionic substances. Non-polar substances are not soluble in polar solvents.

questions.question: The molar solubility of Silver Chromate (Ag₂CrO₄) is $6.5 \times 10^{-5}\text{ M}$. Calculate the $K_{sp}$.
questions.explanation:
Ag₂CrO₄ dissociates as: Ag₂CrO₄(s) ⇌ 2Ag⁺(aq) + CrO₄²⁻(aq)
Ksp = [Ag⁺]²[CrO₄²⁻] = (2s)² × s = 4s³
Ksp = 4 × (6.5 × 10⁻⁵)³ = 4 × 2.746 × 10⁻¹³ = 1.10 × 10⁻¹²

questions.question: Which forces affect solubility?
questions.explanation: Solubility is primarily determined by the intermolecular forces between solute and solvent molecules. Intramolecular forces (bonds within molecules) are not broken during dissolution.

questions.question: Which of the following is the least soluble in water?
questions.explanation: Silver acetate is slightly soluble (moderately soluble) in water. Lead(II) nitrate and magnesium sulfide are soluble, and lithium phosphate is soluble because Li⁺ is a Group 1 ion. Silver acetate has the lowest solubility among these options.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered net ionic equations and solution classification! 🧪🔬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review how to identify spectator ions and calculate solubility limits.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on distinguishing between saturated, unsaturated, and supersaturated solutions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review net ionic equations, solubility rules, and solution classification concepts.
